participants.extracted: Forty-one admitted patients from the psychiatric ward of Noor Hospital in Isfahan, Iran, were randomized; the sample had a mean age of 31.65 years (range 22–43) and included adults aged 15–60 years with recent-onset psychotic symptoms (within 30 days) and DSM-IV-TR diagnoses of schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, schizophreniform disorder, substance-induced psychotic disorder, psychotic disorder due to a general medical condition, or psychotic disorder not otherwise specified. All participants met PANSS positive-symptom inclusion criteria and provided written informed consent. Patients with serious medical illness, recent antipsychotic exposure, long-acting antipsychotic use, mood-disorder-related psychosis, pregnancy or nursing, severe prior drug reactions, or concomitant anticonvulsant, antidepressant, or mood stabilizer use were excluded.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to oral olanzapine, oral risperidone, oral haloperidol, or oral thiothixene for 2 weeks. The comparative focus for this review is oral haloperidol versus oral olanzapine. Doses were approximately chlorpromazine-equivalent 300 mg/day, corresponding to haloperidol 5 mg/day and olanzapine 10 mg/day. All participants could receive biperiden for extrapyramidal symptoms or prophylaxis according to age and prior side effects.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure onset of antipsychotic response using the positive subscales of the Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale (PANSS; items P1–P6: delusion, conceptual disorganization, hallucinatory behavior, excitement, grandiosity, and suspiciousness/persecution). Assessments were scheduled at baseline and then every 2 days during the 2-week treatment period; at each follow-up, response was defined as a score of at least 3 on all positive subscales or a decline of at least 30% in the total positive-subscale score. The study also planned to measure clinical and laboratory safety outcomes at the first screening and the last visit, including extrapyramidal symptoms using the Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale (AIMS/NIMH scale) and other adverse effects such as sedation, blurry vision, dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, and rash. In addition, complete blood count, electrocardiography, aspartate transaminase, alanine transaminase, fasting blood sugar, blood pressure, body weight, and body mass index were monitored at baseline and at the final visit.
